summary: I am Mike Pelletier, born in Old Town, Maine in 1873. I have lived here all my life, working mostly in the Great Works pulp mill since 1887 after finishing school. I have a large family with 13 children, many of whom work in various places like Berlin, New Hampshire and Pittsburgh. Despite my age, I keep my enthusiasm and enjoy life with my wife. I am proud of my French Canadian heritage and my deep roots in this community.

Old Town, Maine is where I was born and have lived all my life. It’s a place with a rich French Canadian community, where my father settled after coming from Saint-Hubert, Quebec in 1865. The town has changed over the years, with streets like Perkins Avenue and areas once swampy now filled in. It’s also where I worked in the pulp mill and raised my family. Old Town has a strong connection to the lumber and pulp industries that shaped our lives.

My father was born in Saint-Hubert, Quebec before moving to Old Town, Maine in 1865. He came from there to seek work and better opportunities. Though I was born in Maine, my roots trace back to Saint-Hubert, and that heritage has influenced my family’s culture and language. It was common for many French Canadians like my father to migrate south for work in the mills. Saint-Hubert represents the origin of our family’s journey to Maine.

One of my youngest boys works in a Civilian Conservation Corps camp, which is a new kind of work for our family. The CCC is a government program that provides jobs, and it’s interesting to see the younger generation involved in it. While I worked in the pulp mill all my life, my children have branched out into different fields, including the CCC. It shows how times are changing from my youth to theirs.

One of my sons works at a filling station in Berlin, New Hampshire. Berlin is a place where some of my family members have found employment outside of Maine. It’s part of the broader region where French Canadians and others seek work in mills and related industries. Though I stayed in Old Town, my family’s reach extends to places like Berlin. It’s good to see my children making their own way in different towns.

Another one of my boys works in a filter plant in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. Pittsburgh is quite far from Old Town, but it’s a city known for its industrial opportunities. It’s remarkable how my children have spread out to places like Pittsburgh to find work in factories and plants. This shows the mobility of our family in search of livelihoods beyond Maine. I’m proud that they have found steady jobs in such important industrial centers.

One of my sons works as a mail clerk in Pittsfield, Massachusetts. Pittsfield is another place where my family members have found work, showing how we have spread across New England and beyond. Though I stayed in Old Town, my children have taken jobs in various towns, including Pittsfield. It’s good to know they are employed and making a life for themselves. This reflects the changing opportunities for our family over the years.

After the boom closed in 1887, I went to work at the Great Works pulp mill, where I have worked ever since. Great Works is a key place in my life and livelihood, central to the pulp and paper industry in Maine. The mill has been my steady employer for decades, and many in the community rely on it. It’s also connected to the church that was moved from Great Works to Old Town in 1870. Great Works represents the industrial backbone of this region during my lifetime.

Though I worked mostly in the pulp mill, I spent one season working on the boom, which was related to the Penobscot Log Driving Association. The log driving was an important part of the lumber industry here, moving logs down the river. It was hard work and seasonal, but it connected me to the broader timber operations in the region. The association helped organize the log drives that were vital to the mills like Great Works. It was a significant experience early in my working life.

My father worked near what is now Perkins Avenue, which used to be the route of the Yeast Railroad. The railroad ran along this street before the tracks were removed and the road was leveled. Perkins Avenue has a history tied to the lumber and transport industries, with the old railroad built by Samuel Yeast. It’s part of the area where my family lived and worked in Old Town. This street holds memories of the early industrial days in our town.

The Yeast Railroad was built by Samuel Yeast and ran between the sawmill and Old Town along what is now Perkins Avenue. It was a narrow, elevated track with wooden rails topped with iron strips, used to transport lumber. My father worked in the sawmill near this railroad when he first came to Old Town. The railroad was eventually removed and the road leveled, but it played a big role in the lumber industry of the area. It’s a piece of local history connected to my family’s early years here.
cues: railroad between sawmill and Old Town

summary: Bangor, Maine is mentioned as a nearby city in the region, connected to Old Town and the surrounding communities. While I lived in Old Town, Bangor was part of the larger area where many French Canadians and others worked and lived. It’s known for its mills and industrial activity, similar to Old Town. Though my life centered in Old Town, Bangor was part of the regional network of towns tied to lumber and pulp industries. It represents the broader economic landscape of our part of Maine.

During World War I, there was an influenza pandemic that affected our community, though thankfully not as severely as earlier epidemics. The war years brought many changes, including health challenges and social events like my granddaughter’s wedding reception. The war period was a time of hardship but also community gatherings and resilience. It was during this time that medical knowledge improved, reducing the death toll from diseases. World War I marks an important era in the history of Old Town and my family’s life.

There was a smallpox epidemic in Old Town about forty years ago, with a pesthouse set up near the old trotting park. It was a serious health crisis in our community, though medical advances have since made such outbreaks less deadly. I remember stories of how these diseases affected families and the town. Smallpox was one of several epidemics that shaped the health history of Old Town. It reminds me of how far medicine has come in my lifetime.

The influenza pandemic of 1918 coincided with the World War I period and caused illness in our community, though not as many deaths as earlier epidemics. It was a frightening time, but medical knowledge had improved by then, helping to reduce fatalities. The pandemic affected many families, including mine, and was part of the broader challenges during the war years. It’s remembered as a significant event in Old Town’s history. The experience showed the importance of health care advancements.

Father Trudel was the priest who arrived in Old Town after the church was moved here from Great Works in 1870. He built the addition on the church and christened me, which makes him a significant figure in my life. He was one of the early priests serving multiple parishes, sometimes traveling far to minister. Father Trudel represents the spiritual leadership of our French Canadian Catholic community. His work helped shape the religious life of Old Town during my youth.

Father O'Brien was an Irish priest who served the northern parish around the time Father Trudel was here. He was one of several priests, including Nicoll and Aspet, who had to cover multiple parishes, sometimes traveling long distances. Though I was baptized by Father Trudel, Father O'Brien’s presence was part of the religious landscape of our area. He represents the diversity of clergy serving the community in those early days. His work helped maintain the Catholic faith among the people of Old Town and beyond.

Father Guillemette is mentioned as one of the early priests serving the area along with Father Trudel and Father O'Brien. Like the others, he had responsibilities over several parishes, sometimes traveling great distances to minister to the faithful. His role was important in supporting the French Canadian Catholic community in Old Town and surrounding towns. Though I did not have personal interaction with him, he was part of the spiritual leadership during my early years. He helped keep the church active and connected to its people.
